review_text: I read the book and found it to be terrible. I'm surprised it was ever published, given the numerous inaccuracies it contained, which made it difficult to read. It's baffling that a movie was made based on such untruths. The praise for the film makes me question how unaware people are of history. I expected much more from this work. I hope they do better next time so that people can learn what really happened.

Elegantissimo film difficile da inserire in un qualsiasi genere, in cui senza l'uso di una sola immagine atroce, riesce perfettamente a spiegare il contesto ed il genocidio in atto a pochissimi passi dalla famiglia rappresentata. Dialoghi interessanti e personaggi diversi tra di loro, in grado di completare un'ampia gamma di comportamenti e di posizioni legate alla guerra. Il film vive di relatività, alcune storie sono volutamente introdotte ma non sviluppate, semplici episodi nella vita di tutti i giorni. Il finale forse è l'unica parte veramente troppo metaforica; con un balzo di 80 anni i personaggi vengono lasciati senza una fine o una morale; lasciando forse un po' di amaro in bocca. Comunque film incredibilmente originale che merita tutto il successo che ha avuto.

One of the most powerful films about the banality of evil ever made. Sandra Hüller's' performance as Hedwig Höss is especially chilling. A mesmerising film that leaves you unsettled and horrified at what humans are capable of. It's particularly pertinent in today's global political landscape and a potent reminder that atrocities aren't committed by monsters, just by ordinary people.
A film describing the banal domestic life of the Commandant of Auschwitz with occasional obscure interludes. The horror of the camp is only reflected in the background sound. Overall disappointing.
Great filming and the sound track definitely had me on edge. It was a bit too slow for me. I probably wouldn't recommend it.

A true horror drama, enjoyable watch but lacked a purpose to the film. One you had to really concentrate on with subtitles. A sad reality to what the jews went through at Auschwitz and the family life behind the scenes.
Borefest. 1:45 minutes before the first REMOTELY interesting thing happened. Same story told a million times this time extra boring.

This could be the most boring movie to you, but there is no denying how horrifying and eerie this movie is throughout the entire runtime. While most of the focus on the film is just everyday life of a family, it is a family that gets their nice life off the blood, death, and torture of the horrifying sounds you hear in the background the entire movie. Probably a must watch.

This film I watched, for the very first time, at HOME theatre situated in Manchester, UK. The film's sound design is so extremely important when watching this particularly film I think. The background sounds carry absolutely all of it!!! The sounds of the train carriages that are continuously pulling up into the station/the sounds of dogs barking/the sounds of gunfire way off in the distance/the sounds of the gas chambers all provide a very disturbing and extremely surreal backdrop to all of the visuals. The scenes of the family enjoying themselves whilst they're in their own back garden and the wife enjoying looking at all of the flowers whilst she is carrying her youngest child in her arms. The background sounds provide the viewer/listener with this earth shatteringly, very deep and completely profound contrast between what defines both the peaceful and the chaotic. It is so utterly understated and yet so extremely skillful artistically (within its composition) that it really succeeds in being able to ignite a very high level of real and genuine disturbance aswell as bringing a wide variety of extremely uncomfortable emotions to the surface aswell. Emotions which I have honestly never experienced before whilst watching a film and don't think that I ever will again either. The film tries to tackles questions such as: ● If you don't believe that what you are doing is actually wrong, would you continue to remain absolutely oblivious to all of it? ● Are human beings able to ignore the plights and the struggles of other people if they are absolutely adamant that their own beliefs and morals are honourable and righteous and the right ones? A very well thought out and also incredibly provocative piece of cinema viewing that absolutely everybody should watch (at least once) within own their lifetimes.
Cerebral, slow and chilling. The industralized and depersonalized evil is pictured. The personal screams, ash and smells form a background and are completely ignored by Herr und Frau Höss.
Best sound design in years. Amazing film.

A film about a banal family in a banal 1930s home - except, of course, that it's right next to Auschwitz. The horrors next door are heard but not seen, and even then only as background noises, but somehow they are still horrifying. Those noises are the story. The Höß family, Rudolf (the commandant) excepted, seem blissfully unaware of the camp over the garden wall, except for when the young son hears some voices very close by and can't fight his curiosity; he looks out of the window just as the guards are drowning a prisoner. He backs away, warning himself "don't do that again." One niggle; the film is in German and the subtitles are awful - white letters often on white backgrounds (buildings, snow etc). This really affected my experience.
Unmissable it’ll stick with you.
Cant recommended. Nothing happens its just the perspective of a German family

Dull, very ‘artistic’ minimalist presentation, where the characters are like plastic figures. You can make the same type of movie about any job. If you look at our current times and the issues with mass migration and governments ignoring the complaints by the people it, along with looming wars … the whole rise of extreme movements that people choose for become far more understandable … conflicts of interest … but then they serve us a movie like this where Nazi’s are plastic emotionless cartoon figures. Nothing can be learned from a movie like this one.
“If you have anything worthwhile saying, don’t bore people.” — Billy Wilder
Love movies based around ww2, holocaust, whatever it be, but this one just wasn’t it. All around boring bleak film. And I enjoy slow burn bleak films.

I found this incredibly boring, I understand that the movie is trying to show how the German family lived an ordinary live while being responsible for killing thousands of people. But the way movie is filmed, the long boring scenes of nothing really bored me.
Well done but still left us with questions about some characters and the magnitude of evilness of the camp.

The film tries to present a different perspective on the horror of Auschwitz, but unfortunately forgets to tell a compelling story. As a result, the promising approach fizzles out into a very slow and tedious film, which, although featuring good actors, offers no tension whatsoever.
One of the Best Films of All Time.

Hauntingly sad and poignant. The unsettling feeling I had as a seemingly normal family lives their everyday life set to the sights and sounds of unadulterated horror and tragedy next door will keep me thinking about this film for a long time.
10/10 movie concept, sound/music, acting and set pieces, but the story was a 5/10. The last 30 minutes take you away from what made the rest of the movie special, and the ending was not for me.
A very well done film although it was hard to follow at times - it seemed a bit "disconnected".
Great movie! It was interesting and thought provoking.

I felt that the deliberate feeling of emptiness that The Zone Of Interest went for detracted from the experience rather than enhancing it. We're very much an outsider looking in on the story here, but I never felt fully gripped since Glazer makes it impossible to get into the minds of the people here.
Normalized hate and violence, yes. Moving depiction of it...for me, no.

For many, the biggest star in The Zone of Interest is the sound, and not without good reason - the movie wouldn't work without the brilliant soundscape. But the documentary-style camera and the editing, which conveys a sense of real time, also contribute to the dense, subliminally eerie atmosphere. However, viewers may occasionally run the risk of succumbing to the profanity of the scenes depicted, as the subtlety of the effect of misdirected attention wears off relatively quickly.
I'm not sure how anyone can even call this a movie. This is the equivalent to a pretentious restaurant serving you a "deconstructed movie". This is not a movie. It should not be considered a movie.

Zone of Interest is a film that will rely on the viewers background knowledge to fill in most of the "horrors". Based of the audio design and the sharp aesthetic this is where most of the praise will land. However past this the film is content on the small everyday problems a family might face. It fails to establish any meaningful characters or give the viewer anything to chew on for the conflict. Do you really care which house this family ends up in? This film is simply too slow and too dull with the subject matter seeming to garner more praise rather than the films execution.

The Zone of Interest is the inversion of every Holocaust film you’ve seen. All we see of Auschwitz are the front gates and the roofs of the buildings from over the wall that separates the camp from the Höss’ property. A sprawling garden, beautiful flowers and paths, a gazebo, a pool, and a greenhouse all lie in the shadow of ignored horrors unfolding merely feet away. As Hedwig (Rudolf’s wife) says, “The vines will grow and cover it all.” The horror of the film is what lies just out of sight the whole time. CONTINUE READING ON LETTERBOXD: https://boxd.it/603YtV

Whilst I can fully appreciate what the film is trying to do in terms of showing the mundane lives of the family managing the camp while unspeakable horrors take place on the other side of the fence, it was just too dull for me. Not enough is shown of what is actually going on, so you really have to use your imagination and knowledge of the horrors of Auschwitz to contrast with what you're watching. There are some thought-provoking moments, such as the wife of the commandant gleefully talking to her friends over coffee about how she found a diamond hidden in an inmates toothpaste tube, but on the whole I found the film slow, too mundane and not nearly engaging enough. There are far better holocaust movies I'd recommend over this one, such as The Pianist and Schindler's List.

On one hand, The Zone of Interest is a well-done film navigating the seemingly calm daily routine of a Nazi’s family home life , as the sounds of gunshots and screams are heard as ordinarily as birds chirping or wind blowing. The film dwells hard on the unsettling reality that these people are also immune and unencumbered by such horrors, as the music seemingly is the apparent factor in portraying the bad. On the other hand, dead-end and long pointless shots make this an extremely slow burn. In fact the wick burned, the smoke faded and all that’s left is a hardened pool of wax after waiting to get from scene to scene. A film does not need to be “woah!” “Look at me!”, but it feels pretentious and unbearably boring. You could have the same premise for any horrifying event in history, and play it to the tune of what’s happening on the other side of the wall, where the eyes don’t meet the action.
This was a well-made, well-attended movie. The movie was not visually horrifying, but the sounds and audio tell so much about what's going on.
